skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use inference.sh CLI commands to discover and run cloud AI apps for images, video, LLMs, search, 3D, and Twitter/X automation.
skill.summary: Run and manage inference.sh cloud AI apps from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: skillssh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external-command, network, filesystem, and environment-variable patterns. Review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, or credential exfiltration evidence; most hits are Markdown CLI examples, but remote installer execution and cloud file uploads require a publication warning.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The documentation repeatedly shows downloading https://cli.inference.sh and piping it directly to sh. This is a true positive dangerous pattern because a compromised endpoint, DNS path, or network layer could execute arbitrary code on the user machine. Context shows it is an installer workflow and SKILL.md provides a manual alternative, so this is not confirmed malicious.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl-to-sh pattern appears in multiple installation examples. The surrounding text presents legitimate CLI installation guidance, which lowers malicious-confidence but not execution-risk confidence.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Upload of Local Files
medium_findings.description: The skill explains that local paths are automatically uploaded when an app input accepts a URL. This is expected behavior for media apps, but it creates a real data exposure risk if users pass private images, audio, video, or paths by mistake.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly document automatic local-file upload to cloud apps. The behavior is intentional and user-driven, but it can expose sensitive local data.

medium_findings.title: External Cloud Actions Through infsh CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands and documents running cloud apps for LLMs, search, media generation, and Twitter/X actions. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but commands can send data to external services and perform account side effects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and examples clearly enable external CLI operations. The commands match the advertised purpose, so this is operational risk rather than evidence of abuse.

low_findings.title: API Key Environment Variable Guidance
low_findings.description: The references document INFSH_API_KEY for CI/CD and troubleshooting. This is normal authentication guidance, but users should avoid exposing keys in prompts, shell history, saved files, or logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly references an API key environment variable. There is no evidence that the skill reads or exfiltrates the key itself.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Mostly Document Vendor Resources
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, assets, installer distribution, and result examples. No evidence found that these URLs are stealth exfiltration endpoints, although the installer URL is separately covered as an execution risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL context is documentation, imagery, installation, or expected cloud-result output. No hidden endpoint or credential transmission pattern appears in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography at several Markdown lines, but review found command tables, headings, or non-cryptographic text at those locations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, custom crypto, or insecure verification logic in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain weak cryptographic algorithms. The only nearby checksum claim in SKILL.md describes SHA-256 verification, which is not weak for this purpose.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external-command detections are Markdown inline code or fenced shell examples, not Ruby backtick execution. They still describe commands users may run, but the static label overstates the implementation risk because this skill has no Ruby source code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are Markdown references and SKILL.md content. The cited backticks format command examples rather than invoking Ruby shell execution.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Not Observed
low_findings.description: No evidence found of text that asks the assistant to ignore instructions, override review rules, claim preapproval, or change the audit outcome. The files read as product documentation for the inference.sh CLI.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed headings and instructions describe CLI usage. I did not find system-message impersonation or review-bypass language in the analyzed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Script to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: Installer examples use a network download piped directly into sh. Prefer manual download, checksum verification, and binary installation when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and repeated. It is a known supply-chain risk even when used by legitimate installers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Local File Uploads
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI can upload local paths to cloud apps. Users need review controls before sending files to external model providers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that local paths are accepted and uploaded. The user controls the path, but accidental exposure remains plausible.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key in Environment Variable
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents INFSH_API_KEY for automation. Environment secrets are acceptable but require CI secret storage and log controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The API key variable is documented directly. No evidence shows unauthorized reading or transmission by the skill itself.

content.user_title: Run AI Apps from the CLI
content.value_statement: Teams need one workflow for many AI services without managing separate model APIs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh CLI usage.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install, authenticate, and update the infsh CLI.
Shows how to list, search, filter, and inspect available inference.sh apps.
Guides app runs with input files, inline input, version pins, and no-wait mode.
Documents task status checks and saving task results to files.
Explains local file uploads for supported image, audio, video, and media inputs.
Covers image, video, LLM, search, 3D, utility, and Twitter/X app categories.
limitations: Requires the infsh CLI, an inference.sh account, and valid credentials.
Does not run models locally or remove provider usage limits.
Cloud apps may upload prompts, inputs, and selected local files to external services.
Available apps, pricing, outputs, and provider behavior can change over time.

use_cases.title: Generate Media Assets
use_cases.description: Find and run image, video, avatar, audio, and upscaling apps from one CLI workflow.

use_cases.title: Compare AI Models
use_cases.description: Discover available LLM, search, and media apps, then test prompts with saved input files.

use_cases.title: Automate Publishing Tasks
use_cases.description: Run Twitter/X and media utility apps while tracking long-running tasks and results.

prompt_templates.title: Start Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup on a new machine
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the safest way to install the inference.sh CLI, log in, and confirm that authentication works.

prompt_templates.title: Find an App
prompt_templates.scenario: Search for a useful AI service
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me find an inference.sh app for image generation. Include how to view details and sample input requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Execute an AI app with a prepared input file
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through running a selected inference.sh app with an input file, then checking task status and saving the result.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow that generates an image, turns it into video, stores outputs, and avoids uploading sensitive local files.

output_examples.input: Find an app for image generation.
output: Suggested image app categories and notable app names.
Next step to inspect the app schema before preparing input.
Reminder to avoid private files unless upload is intended.

output_examples.input: Run a long video generation task.
output: Task tracking steps for checking progress later.
Guidance for saving the completed result.
Use an environment variable for the inference.sh API key.
Store the key in the CI secret manager.
Do not print secrets in logs or prompts.

best_practices: Inspect each app schema before sending prompts, files, or credentials.
Use saved input files for repeatable tasks and review them before upload.
Store API keys in a secret manager and keep command output out of logs.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell without reviewing the source or using a safer install path.
Do not pass private local files unless the upload is required and approved.
Do not automate Twitter/X actions without account, policy, and content review controls.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an AI assistant in using the inference.sh CLI to discover and run cloud AI apps.

faq.question: Does it require an account?
faq.answer: Yes. You need an inference.sh account and either CLI login or an API key.

faq.question: Can it upload local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI can upload local paths when an app input accepts files or URLs.

faq.question: Does it execute arbitrary shell commands?
faq.answer: The skill declares Bash access for infsh commands, but its documentation also shows installer commands for users.

faq.question: Is it safe for secrets?
faq.answer: Use a secret manager for API keys and avoid sharing keys in prompts, files, or command output.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
